content:
Is there a way to link to the rustdoc docs at https://rust.docs.kernel.org but to a specific kernel version? I found that https://kernel.org/doc/rustdoc/v6.14 works, but I'm not sure how intended that is.

I probably need to use some of the docs pages as reference in my thesis and I'd like to refer to the actual kernel version I'm using in the thesis (probably v6.14). It doesn't have to be online forever, it just should not change immediately when a new kernel version is published.

Please use e.g. https://rust.docs.kernel.org/6.14/ (only main tags are available)

There is also https://rust.docs.kernel.org/next/ (but this one changes roughly every day)

I hope that helps!

Lukas Fischer said:

I probably need to use some of the docs pages as reference in my thesis and I'd like to refer to the actual kernel version I'm using in the thesis (probably v6.14). It doesn't have to be online forever, it just should not change immediately when a new kernel version is published.

Would it be OK to refer to the source that the docs are generated from?

Miguel Ojeda said:

Please use e.g. https://rust.docs.kernel.org/6.14/ (only main tags are available)

That's the kind of link I was looking for, thanks! Main tags only are fine, I'm not using a non-release version to base my thesis developments on in the end. Currently looks like it'll be 6.14 (with Boqun's RCU changes from here merged), since 6.15 likely releases after I hand in the thesis.

Andreas Hindborg said:

Would it be OK to refer to the source that the docs are generated from?

That would also work (and allow me to link to a tag or commit), I'd prefer to refer to the rendered version though.

Also maybe related question, as "author" for the rustdoc source I'm using "The kernel development community", since that's what https://docs.kernel.org uses for the copyright and not all pages there list authors explicitly, so I already used that for kernel docs sources (unless there are authors listed explicitly). Should I use something else? https://rust-for-linux.com also lists no alternative I could quickly see.

Alternatively I thought I could check in git who's responsible for that part of the docs, since I've done that for some other sources in the past, but I figured this was easier.
It is probably easier to just list "linux kernel contributors" or something like that.

That's the kind of link I was looking for, thanks! Main tags only are fine, I'm not using a non-release version to base my thesis developments on in the end.

Glad it helped!

You could also consider giving the generated HTML of your full tree (i.e. including e.g. Boqun's) as a "complement" artifact for your thesis, if you do that kind of thing at your university/department. They are trivial to generate, after all, with the make .... rustdoc target.

Lukas Fischer said:

Also maybe related question, as "author" for the rustdoc source I'm using "The kernel development community", since that's what https://docs.kernel.org uses for the copyright and not all pages there list authors explicitly, so I already used that for kernel docs sources (unless there are authors listed explicitly). Should I use something else? https://rust-for-linux.com also lists no alternative I could quickly see.

Alternatively I thought I could check in git who's responsible for that part of the docs, since I've done that for some other sources in the past, but I figured this was easier.

It depends on what you mean by "author", I guess.

In some places, we just say "Rust for Linux Contributors" for simplicity, so you can definitely use that.

If you want to be proper, yeah, Git is the place to check. In that case, you may happen to hit the original commits, which have quite a few co-developers, since it was a merge of many things we did in the past, and if I did my job correctly, I hopefully didn't miss anyone... :) If you want to go even earlier in time to see the individual contributors, you would need to check the rust branch in our old out-of-tree repository.

You can also see commit 8aebac82933f ("Merge tag 'rust-v6.1-rc1' of https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux") for an even longer list of contributors (173!), including ones that did not have kernel commits as such.

Miguel Ojeda said:

You could also consider giving the generated HTML of your full tree (i.e. including e.g. Boqun's) as a "complement" artifact for your thesis, if you do that kind of thing at your university/department. They are trivial to generate, after all, with the make .... rustdoc target.

I plan to publish the thesis (unless the result is very unsatisfactory, which at its current progress seems unlikely), and I also plan to include the code as an artifact, for that it might be a good idea to include the generated docs as well.

For the document itself I'm not sure how exactly I would refer to something in the thesis artifacts, there's probably a decent way to do that. In this specific case I just needed a source to attach to the claim that at the state I'm using it, Rust for Linux only provides spinlocks, mutexes or RCU as synchronization/locking mechanisms.

To hand in the thesis I only need to provide the printed version and a digital (non pdf) version, we don't have a way to handle thesis artifacts within the university. My code lives in the university GitLab, so my group already has access.

Miguel Ojeda said:

It depends on what you mean by "author", I guess.

I would say the default meaning of "author" in the context of a source for scientific work: who produced the referenced work.

In some places, we just say "Rust for Linux Contributors" for simplicity, so you can definitely use that.

If you want to be proper, yeah, Git is the place to check.

Which of both I use usually depends on how simple it is to get the individual authors, usually I prefer the latter, but for the other kernel docs I did not always want to dive into a git blame of the relevant document, so I figured a generic term is usually fine in that regard. Thanks for the suggestion!
Lukas Fischer has marked this topic as resolved.
